name: Maglić (2386m) — Bosnia's Highest Peak, from Prijevor
description: Bosnia & Herzegovina's highest summit (2386m) in Sutjeska NP, climbed from the Prijevor saddle (~1668m). Short on the map (2–2.5km, ~700m gain) but the direct NW-face route is a steep, scree-and-rock scramble with a fixed-cable/via-ferrata crux and serious exposure — the most demanding hike on this whole trip.
communityNotes: EXPOSURE — honest read: the direct 'Alpine Route' from Prijevor is the crux. Budget Bucket List: 'you're climbing, with hand and feet, sliding passed the steep rocks along crackling cables... pretty hardcore'; and bluntly, 'if you're not unfamiliar with vertigo or doubt your level of fitness, then this is definitely a big no-go' — she recommends going UP only on this route, not down. A 2025 first-hand winter climb: 'Slipping down would mean going down several hundred meters' and 'some of the rocks which looked like solid handles were breaking in our hands.' A route guide warns the cable's 'anchor points have not been maintained for a very long time and are of questionable safety.' Npsutjeska's own sign: 'Very steep, rocky and exposed... hike this section with helmet and good hiking shoes only.' ALTERNATE FOR THE EXPOSURE-AVERSE MEMBER: don't take the direct face. The route up via Trnovačko Lake (the 'Lake Route') is 'more gradual and bearable, mellow, relatively flat sections before inclining toward the summit' — classic combo is 'walk the Alpine Route up and the lake route down' (so the nervous member can do Trnovačko + turn back, or ascend the gentler lake side). Times: direct ~1.5–3h up / the lake-side ascent 5–7h. JUNE SNOW: a Feb attempt hit snow 1km below Prijevor; by mid-June the 2300m+ upper slopes are usually clear but check — 'Maglić' literally means 'misty', fog descends fast and the cabled face is no place to be lost. GUIDE/BORDER: no guide legally required, but the summit straddles the BA/ME border and operators run guided trips; bring passports.
cost: Park entry 10 KM (~€5) pp + ~5 KM car/day
timeNeeded: Direct route 4–6h RT; via-lake ascent makes it a full day
bestTime: Start early; July/Aug snow-free but go before midday fog. Helmet recommended.

name: Trnovačko Jezero Hike (Heart-Shaped Lake) — from Prijevor
description: The famous heart-shaped glacial lake (1517m) below Maglić — the standout easier hike of Sutjeska. ~5km / ~2–2.5h one way from the Prijevor saddle, moderate, with a casual on-foot border crossing into Montenegro. BORDER QUIRK: the lake is physically in Montenegro (Piva NP, Plužine) but accessed from the Bosnian/Sutjeska side — verified by OSM (display name reads 'Opština Plužine, Crna Gora').
communityNotes: The accessible-to-everyone option vs Maglić — same trailhead, no exposure. 'The hike is about 2.5 hours one way and the trail itself is not that interesting imo but very easy. Worth it once you reach the lake.' BORDER: you literally walk across the BA→ME line on the trail — a hiker describes the marker as just 'a dead tree' and you 'simply cross the border without border control, without waiting, without a passport' (carry ID/passport anyway, it's a real international border). CAMP/SWIM: wild camping is normal — 'in summer many hikers pitch tents on the meadows around the lake'; there's a seasonal mountain café/cottage (ranger Miloš) with food and reportedly water. Swimming: lovely in Jul/Aug (up to ~22°C); in June it's cold but 'possible for the brave'. One real gotcha: 'thousands of mosquitoes spiced it up a little bit' at the lake. June = quiet/uncrowded but weather less predictable. This is the perfect plan-B that survives a 'we're tired' day: do the lake, skip the summit.
cost: Park entry 10 KM (~€5) pp + ~5 KM car/day; free to camp at lake
timeNeeded: ~5h RT (2–2.5h each way); longer if camping overnight
bestTime: June quiet but cold for swimming; mornings for the heart-shape light
facilities: Wild camping on lakeside meadows; seasonal mountain café/cottage with food + water (summer only)

name: Zelengora Lakes (Orlovačko / Štirinsko / Kotlaničko)
description: The gentle, soulful side of Sutjeska NP: a plateau of seven glacial lakes ('green mountain') with easy meadow-and-forest walks — the low-stress alternative to Maglić. Orlovačko jezero (1438m, coords here) is the most accessible: you can drive almost to the shore and a short circular route links several lakes (moderate, no exposure).
communityNotes: The relaxed Sutjeska option — 'lakes, viewpoints, meadows and silence.' Orlovačko 'is the most accessible and the most photographed... you only need to descend on foot, but it isn't far, nor strenuous.' A multi-lake loop (Orlovačko–Štirinsko–Kotlaničko/Jugovo) is rated 'moderate, physical fitness required but previous experience is not necessary... a smooth circular route at a relaxed mountain pace.' Bregoč summit (2015m) is ~1h40 'not technically demanding.' From Donje Bare it's 30 min on foot to the Borić viewpoint, 'the finest viewpoint on all of Zelengora' (Sutjeska canyon + Maglić behind). ROAD REALITY — the catch: the access roads (R434 from Foča/Kalinovik/Gacko) are 'totally unpaved... requiring a 4x4', a 'bumpy, dusty ride of about an hour'; Donje Bare is 'accessible by off-road vehicles only.' A regular rental car likely can't reach the trailheads in the wet — plan a 4x4 transfer (operators run them from Foča/Tjentište) or pick a dry day. June: 'lush green', wildflowers; swimming and camping permitted at the lakes.
cost: Park entry 10 KM (~€5) pp; 4x4 transfer extra if no off-road car
timeNeeded: Half day (single lake) to full day (multi-lake loop)
bestTime: June for green meadows + flowers; pick a dry day for the dirt road
facilities: Lakeside wild camping; Orlovačko has a mountain hut; swimming OK

name: Perućica Primeval Forest + Skakavac (75m) — Vidikovac Viewpoint
description: Europe's last great primeval forest (1434 ha, UNESCO tentative) in Sutjeska NP, with the 75m Skakavac waterfall tumbling through it. NOTE: this is the SUTJESKA Skakavac, NOT the 98m one near Sarajevo already in the data. A ~100m path from the Dragoš sedlo parking reaches the Vidikovac viewpoint over the whole forest and falls — open to everyone, no guide. Entering the forest itself is guide-only (16 people/day max).
communityNotes: Two very different experiences. (1) VIDIKOVAC viewpoint: easy — 'a hundred-metre-long path leads from the parking place in Dragoš sedlo to Vidikovac... the view to Perućica and Skakavac waterfall, which falls down from a height of 75 metres' — anyone can do this, it's basically a roadside stop on the way up to Prijevor. (2) INTO the forest: 'access is only permitted with a certified national-park guide, and only 16 people per day' (book ahead via the park). The guided forest+waterfall hike is ~3.5km / ~2–2.5h, 'challenging but rewarding... one of Europe's last untouched primeval forests.' Falls run strongest spring/early summer, so June is good. Pair this with the Prijevor drive (the road to Maglić/Trnovačko passes right by Dragoš sedlo) — the viewpoint is a no-cost, no-effort add-on even on a tired day.
cost: Park entry 10 KM (~€5) pp; guided forest entry extra (book via park)
bestTime: June for strong waterfall flow; book guide ahead for forest entry

name: Prenj — Zelena Glava (2155m), 'Himalayas of Herzegovina'
description: The highest peak of wild Prenj near Konjic — dramatic, alpine, and the most committing mountain in Herzegovina. HONEST VERDICT FOR THIS GROUP: not a casual day hike. It's doable in a day from the high Tisovica valley (~1450m) but involves a final via-ferrata/cable scramble to the summit, fog-and-navigation risk on a karst 'labyrinth', and Prenj's notorious lack of water. A guide is strongly recommended; the exposure-averse member would likely dislike the summit pitch.
communityNotes: REALISTIC ACCESSIBILITY: 'Hiking the high peaks of Prenj is generally not recommended for absolute beginners without a guide.' From Tisovica it's ~2–3h moderate walking, then 'once you arrive under the Zelena Glava cliff, the next hour will be a steep climb, at places using ferrata until you are at the top' — a 'secured cable route (via ferrata) that tests both nerve and fitness.' So: fit non-alpinists CAN do it as a long day from Tisovica, but it's the technical crux, not a stroll. WATER (verified — carry a LOT): 'Prenj is dry and thirsty, swallowing rain into subterranean channels... carry at least 3–4 litres of water per person per day, springs are rare and often dry up in summer.' One spring exists ~150m left of the trail at the edge of Tisovica valley, and you can sometimes refill at the Jezerce hut — don't rely on either. NAVIGATION: 'fog can descend rapidly on Prenj, turning the limestone labyrinth into a dangerous trap' — GPS + guide advised. JUNE: 'before June, snow can still block the higher passes'; by mid-late June usually clearing but verify. ROAD: Tisovica trailhead needs a 'robust off-road vehicle... rough macadam road, unsuitable for smaller vehicles' — a normal rental CAN'T get up; use a Konjic operator's 4x4 transfer. Gentler alternative start: Rujište (1050m, asphalt, 25km N of Mostar) but that's a much longer ascent. WAR-ERA NOTE: 'some remote areas of Prenj still carry a risk of unexploded ordnance' — stick strictly to marked trails.
cost: Free trail; 4x4 transfer to Tisovica via Konjic operator (paid)
timeNeeded: Long full day from Tisovica; or overnight at Jezerce hut
bestTime: Late June onward (snow clears); start at dawn, carry 3–4L water pp

name: Bjelašnica Summit (2067m) from Babin Do
description: A short, easy summit near the Olympic ski resort — distinct from the existing Umoljani→Lukomir rim hike. From the Babin Do ski base you climb open ski-slope/ridge terrain to the 2067m summit topped by the old Olympic observatory. Quick, no exposure, big 360° views — a low-commitment 'bag a 2000er' add-on if based around Bjelašnica/Lukomir.
communityNotes: The contrast to the long Umoljani–Lukomir canyon-rim hike already in the data: this is the short, sharp summit option from the resort side. From Babin Do the ascent is roughly 1.5–2h up grassy/rocky slopes (the ski infrastructure means it's never remote or technical), summit marked by the abandoned Olympic weather observatory. No scrambling or exposure — fine for the whole group including the heights-averse member, and easy to bail on. Best as a half-day or sunrise jaunt if you're already overnighting near Bjelašnica/Lukomir; otherwise the Umoljani–Lukomir rim hike is the more rewarding outing. Road to Babin Do is paved (it's a developed resort), so a normal car is fine — the easiest trailhead access of any hike here.
bestTime: Clear day for the 360° panorama; sunrise option
